{{short description|Social psychologist}}

| native_name = יעקב טרופ

'''Yaacov Trope''' ([[Hebrew language|Hebrew]]: יעקב טרופ; born June 17, 1945)<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=CURRICULUM VITAE Yaacov Trope New York University |url=https://dokumen.tips/documents/curriculum-vitae-yaacov-trope-new-york-university-cv-5-16-2012016-06-21curriculum.html |access-date=2022-11-28 |website=dokumen.tips |language=en}}</ref> is a social [[psychologist]] who studies cognitive, motivational, and social factors that enable perspective taking, and effects of emotions and desires on [[Social judgment theory|social judgment]] and [[Decision-making|decision making]]. He is a Professor of Psychology at [[New York University]].

Trope won the 2016 Mentor Award from the [[Association for Psychological Science]] for his outstanding mentorship and support for psychology students.<ref>{{Cite web |title=2016 APS Mentor Award |url=https://www.psychologicalscience.org/members/awards-and-honors/aps-mentor-award/mentor-award-recipients/trope |access-date=2022-10-27 |website=Association for Psychological Science – APS |language=en-US}}</ref> In 2015, he won the Career Contribution Award from the [[Society for Personality and Social Psychology]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Career Contribution Award {{!}} Society for Personality and Social Psychology |url=https://spsp.org/membership/awards/seniorcareer/career-contribution-award |access-date=2022-11-28 |website=spsp.org}}</ref> and the [[Tom Ostrom|Thomas M. Ostrom]] Award for Outstanding Contributions to Social Cognition.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ostrom Award |url=https://pmigconference.com/undefined/ostrom-award |access-date=2022-11-28 |website=Home |language=en-US}}</ref> Trope is an elected member of the [[American Academy of Arts and Sciences]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Yaacov Trope |url=https://www.amacad.org/person/yaacov-trope |access-date=2022-11-28 |website=American Academy of Arts & Sciences |language=en}}</ref>

== Biography ==
Trope received his B.A degree in Psychology from [[Tel Aviv University]] in 1970. He attended graduate school at the [[University of Michigan]] where he obtained a M.A in 1972 and a Ph.D in Social Psychology in 1974.<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Arts |first1=-Professor of Psychology – NYU Faculty of |last2=Appointment |first2=Science-Affiliated |last3=Marketing |first3=Department of |title=NYU Stern – Yaacov Trope – Professor of Psychology |url=https://www.stern.nyu.edu/faculty/bio/yaacov-trope |access-date=2022-10-20 |website=www.stern.nyu.edu |language=en}}</ref> As a scholar from Israel, Trope received a [[Fulbright Program|Fulbright fellowship]] to conduct research with [[Eugene Burnstein|Eugene Bernstein]] at the University of Michigan from 1979–1980.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Yaacov Trope {{!}} Fulbright Scholar Program |url=https://fulbrightscholars.org/grantee/yaacov-trope |access-date=2022-11-28 |website=fulbrightscholars.org}}</ref>

Trope was a faculty member at the [[Hebrew University of Jerusalem]] from 1974 to 1989 before joining the faculty of New York University in 1990.  From 1990–1998, Trope had a joint appointment as a Professor of Psychology at Tel Aviv University.<ref name=":0" />

== Research ==
Trope's research examines social and emotional factors in decision-making.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Yaacov Trope: H-index & Awards – Academic Profile |url=https://research.com/u/yaacov-trope |access-date=2022-10-27 |website=Research.com |language=en}}</ref> He is especially interested in the process of [[construal]], or how people perceive, comprehend, and interpret the world and make connections. With his colleague Nira Liberman, Trope developed [[construal level theory]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Trope |first1=Yaacov |last2=Liberman |first2=Nira |date=2010 |title=Construal-level theory of psychological distance. |journal=Psychological Review |language=en |volume=117 |issue=2 |pages=440–463 |doi=10.1037/a0018963 |issn=1939-1471 |pmc=3152826 |pmid=20438233}}</ref> to account for relations between perceived [[psychological distance]] and the extent to which one's thinking about a given topic or situation will tend to more concrete vs. more [[Abstract thinking|abstract]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Theory {{!}} Construal Level Theory |url=https://u.osu.edu/construalleveltheory/theory/ |access-date=2022-12-13 |website=u.osu.edu}}</ref> That is, if a situation that has a direct effect on the person, they will tend to think about the situation in a concrete way and their responses will be more immediate. In contrast, if the situation has only a remote effect on the person, they will tend to think about it more abstractly and reason about it more slowly.

Other work has focused specifically on temporal construal, or how distance in time affects one's perceptions of present and future events.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Templin |first=Neal |title=The Urge to Splurge: Don't Worry, It'll Pass |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB123319605956027285 |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=WSJ |date=29 January 2009 |language=en-US}}</ref> Trope and Liberman draw a contrast between high-level construal of temporally distant events, which captures the bigger picture or the overall main point with a few abstract features, and low-level construal of temporally close events, which includes smaller details that do not play an essential role in the event or are of little importance.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Trope |first1=Yaacov |last2=Liberman |first2=Nira |date=2003 |title=Temporal construal. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0033-295X.110.3.403 |journal=Psychological Review |language=en |volume=110 |issue=3 |pages=403–421 |doi=10.1037/0033-295X.110.3.403 |pmid=12885109 |issn=1939-1471|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Trope's research has examined how people reveal their [[emotions]] via [[facial expression]] and [[body language]], and how facial expressions in response to intensely joyful or painful events may be surprisingly similar.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Study: Facial expressions of intense joy and anguish are indistinguishable {{!}} American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS) |url=https://www.aaas.org/study-facial-expressions-intense-joy-and-anguish-are-indistinguishable |access-date=2022-12-11 |website=www.aaas.org |language=en}}</ref> Trope and his colleagues found that body language provides a more accurate indicator of how one is feeling as compared to facial expressions—a finding that runs counter to what people generally think.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Aviezer |first1=Hillel |last2=Trope |first2=Yaacov |last3=Todorov |first3=Alexander |date=2012-11-30 |title=Body Cues, Not Facial Expressions, Discriminate Between Intense Positive and Negative Emotions |url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.1224313 |journal=Science |language=en |volume=338 |issue=6111 |pages=1225–1229 |doi=10.1126/science.1224313 |pmid=23197536 |bibcode=2012Sci...338.1225A |s2cid=5867506 |issn=0036-8075|url-access=subscription }}</ref> While it is sometimes difficult to read emotions from a person's face alone, it is much easier to deduce how the person is feeling when their bodies are visible.

== Books ==
* Chaiken, S., & Trope, Y. (Eds.) (1999). ''Dual-process theories in social psychology.'' The Guilford Press.
* Trope, Y., & Higgins, E. T. (1993). ''Dispositional inferences from behavior.'' Sage Publications.

== Representative publications ==
* {{cite journal | last1 = Fujita | first1 = K. | last2 = Trope | first2 = Y. | last3 = Liberman | first3 = N. | last4 = Levin-Sagi | first4 = M. | year = 2006 | title = Construal levels and self-control | url = | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 90 | issue = 3| pages = 351–367 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.90.3.351 }}
*{{cite journal | last1 = Liberman | first1 = N. | last2 = Trope | first2 = Y. | year = 1998 | title = The role of feasibility and desirability considerations in near and distant future decisions: A test of temporal construal theory | url = | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 75 | issue = 1| pages = 5–18 | doi = 10.1037/0022-3514.75.1.5 }}
* Trope, Y. (1986). Self-enhancement and self-assessment in achievement behavior. In R. M. Sorrentino & E. T. Higgins (Eds.), ''Handbook of motivation and cognition: Foundations of social behavior'' (pp.&nbsp;350–378). Guilford Press.
* {{cite journal | pmid = 20438233 | doi=10.1037/a0018963 | pmc=3152826 | volume=117 | title=Construal-level theory of psychological distance | year=2010 | journal=Psychol Rev | pages=440–63  | last1 = Trope | first1 = Y | last2 = Liberman | first2 = N | issue=2 }} Erratum in ''Psychological Review, 117''(3), 1024. 
* {{cite journal | last1 = Trope | first1 = Y. | last2 = Liberman | first2 = N | last3 = Wakslak | first3 = C. | year = 2007 | title = Construal levels and psychological distance: Effects on representation, prediction, evaluation, and behavior | journal = Journal of Consumer Psychology | volume = 17 | issue = 2| pages = 83–95 | pmid = 21822366 | doi = 10.1016/S1057-7408(07)70013-X | pmc = 3150814 }}
